    24  // ActivateContentPack Activate content pack.
    25  // Activate a Cloud SIEM content pack. This operation configures the necessary
    26  // log filters or security filters depending on the pricing model and updates the content
    27  // pack activation state.

   112  // AttachCase Attach security findings to a case.
   113  // Attach security findings to a case.
   114  // You can attach up to 50 security findings per case. Security findings that are already attached to another case will be detached from their previous case and attached to the specified case.

   194  // AttachJiraIssue Attach security findings to a Jira issue.
   195  // Attach security findings to a Jira issue by providing the Jira issue URL.
   196  // You can attach up to 50 security findings per Jira issue. If the Jira issue is not linked to any case, this operation will create a case for the security findings and link the Jira issue to the newly created case. To configure the Jira integration, see [Bidirectional ticket syncing with Jira](https://docs.datadoghq.com/security/ticketing_integrations/#bidirectional-ticket-syncing-with-jira). Security findings that are already attached to another Jira issue will be detached from their previous Jira issue and attached to the specified Jira issue.

   275  // AttachServiceNowTicket Attach security findings to a ServiceNow ticket.
   276  // Attach security findings to a ServiceNow ticket by providing the ServiceNow ticket URL.
   277  // You can attach up to 50 security findings per ServiceNow ticket. If the ServiceNow ticket is not linked to any case, this operation will create a case for the security findings and link the ServiceNow ticket to the newly created case. Security findings that are already attached to another ServiceNow ticket will be detached from their previous ServiceNow ticket and attached to the specified ServiceNow ticket.

   455  // BulkConvertExistingSecurityMonitoringRules Bulk convert rules to Terraform.
   456  // Convert a list of existing security monitoring rules to Terraform for the Datadog provider
   457  // resource `datadog_security_monitoring_rule`. Returns a ZIP archive containing one Terraform
   458  // file per rule. You can convert rules for the following types:
   459  // - App and API Protection
   460  // - Cloud SIEM (log detection and signal correlation)
   461  // - Workload Protection

   532  // BulkCreateSampleLogGenerationSubscriptions Bulk subscribe to sample log generation.

   535  // status so partial successes can be inspected.

   977  // BulkExportSecurityMonitoringRules Bulk export security monitoring rules.
   978  // Export a list of security monitoring rules as a ZIP file containing JSON rule definitions.
   979  // The endpoint accepts a list of rule IDs and returns a ZIP archive where each rule is
   980  // saved as a separate JSON file named after the rule.

  1054  // and must be one of `suppressions`, `critical_assets`, `security_filters`, or `rules`.

  1806  // CreateJiraIssues Create Jira issues for security findings.
  1807  // Create Jira issues for security findings.
  1808  // This operation creates a case in Datadog and a Jira issue linked to that case for bidirectional sync between Datadog and Jira. To configure the Jira integration, see [Bidirectional ticket syncing with Jira](https://docs.datadoghq.com/security/ticketing_integrations/#bidirectional-ticket-syncing-with-jira). You can create up to 50 Jira issues per request and associate up to 50 security findings per Jira issue.   1887  // CreateSampleLogGenerationSubscription Subscribe to sample log generation.
  1888  // Subscribe to sample log generation for a Cloud SIEM content pack. Sample logs for the
  1889  // requested content pack are injected into the Logs platform for the duration of the subscription,
  1890  // so detection rules can be exercised without onboarding the underlying integration first.
  1891  //
  1892  // **Availability**: this endpoint is restricted to Cloud SIEM trial organizations on an
  1893  // eligible pricing model. Non-trial orgs receive `403 Forbidden`, the feature flag may also reject

  2789  // CreateServiceNowTickets Create ServiceNow tickets for security findings.
  2790  // Create ServiceNow tickets for security findings.
  2791  // This operation creates a case in Datadog and a ServiceNow ticket linked to that case for bidirectional sync between Datadog and ServiceNow. You can create up to 50 ServiceNow tickets per request and associate up to 50 security findings per ServiceNow ticket. Security findings that are already attached to another ServiceNow ticket will be detached from their previous ServiceNow ticket and attached to the newly created ServiceNow ticket.

  4365  // DetachCase Detach security findings from their case.
  4366  // Detach security findings from their case.
  4367  // This operation dissociates security findings from their associated cases without deleting the cases themselves. You can detach security findings from multiple different cases in a single request, with a limit of 50 security findings per request.   4769  // ExportSecurityMonitoringTerraformResource Export security monitoring resource to Terraform.
  4770  // Export a security monitoring resource to a Terraform configuration.
  4771  // The `resource_type` path parameter specifies the type of resource to export
  4772  // and must be one of `suppressions`, `critical_assets`, `security_filters`, or `rules`.
  4773  // For `rules`, partner rules cannot be exported and return a 400 error.

  8816  // ImportSecurityVulnerabilities Import security vulnerabilities.
  8817  // Import security vulnerabilities from an external scanner in CycloneDX 1.5 format.
  8818  //
  8819  // The payload is validated against the CycloneDX 1.5 JSON schema and the following
  8820  // additional constraints:
  8821  //
  8822  //   - `metadata`, `metadata.component`, and `metadata.component.name` are required.
  8823  //   - `metadata.tools.components` must contain exactly one element with a `name` field.
  8824  //   - `components` cannot be empty. Each component requires `bom-ref`, `type`, `name`, and `version`.
  8825  //   - When `type` is `library`, `purl` is required and must be a valid PURL.
  8826  //   - When `type` is `operating-system`, `name` must be one of the supported OS values:
  8827  //     `alma`, `alpine`, `amazon`, `azurelinux`, `bottlerocket`, `cbl-mariner`, `chainguard`,
  8828  //     `centos`, `debian`, `fedora`, `opensuse`, `opensuse-leap`, `opensuse-tumbleweed`,
  8829  //     `oracle`, `photon`, `redhat`, `rocky`, `slem`, `sles`, `ubuntu`, `wolfi`, `windows`, `macos`.
  8830  //   - `vulnerabilities` cannot be empty. Each vulnerability requires `id`, exactly one `ratings` entry,
  8831  //     and at least one `affects` entry.
  8832  //   - Each `affects[].ref` must match a `bom-ref` value in `components`.
  8833  func (a *SecurityMonitoringApi) ImportSecurityVulnerabilities(ctx _context.Context, body CycloneDXBom) (*_nethttp.Response, error) {

  9221  // ListFindings List findings.
  9222  // Get a list of findings. These include both misconfigurations and identity risks.
  9223  //
  9224  // **Note**: To filter and return only identity risks, add the following query parameter: `?filter[tags]=dd_rule_type:ciem`
  9225  //
  9226  // ### Filtering
  9227  //
  9228  // Filters can be applied by appending query parameters to the URL.
  9229  //
  9230  //   - Using a single filter: `?filter[attribute_key]=attribute_value`
  9231  //   - Chaining filters: `?filter[attribute_key]=attribute_value&filter[attribute_key]=attribute_value...`
  9232  //   - Filtering on tags: `?filter[tags]=tag_key:tag_value&filter[tags]=tag_key_2:tag_value_2`
  9233  //
  9234  // Here, `attribute_key` can be any of the filter keys described further below.
  9235  //
  9236  // Query parameters of type `integer` support comparison operators (`>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`). This is particularly useful when filtering by `evaluation_changed_at` or `resource_discovery_timestamp`. For example: `?filter[evaluation_changed_at]=>20123123121`.
  9237  //
  9238  // You can also use the negation operator on strings. For example, use `filter[resource_type]=-aws*` to filter for any non-AWS resources.
  9239  //
  9240  // The operator must come after the equal sign. For example, to filter with the `>=` operator, add the operator after the equal sign: `filter[evaluation_changed_at]=>=1678809373257`.
  9241  //
  9242  // Query parameters must be only among the documented ones and with values of correct types. Duplicated query parameters (e.g. `filter[status]=low&filter[status]=info`) are not allowed.
  9243  //
  9244  // ### Additional extension fields
  9245  //
  9246  // Additional extension fields are available for some findings.
  9247  //
  9248  // The data is available when you include the query parameter `?detailed_findings=true` in the request.
  9249  //
  9250  // The following fields are available for findings:
  9251  // - `external_id`: The resource external ID related to the finding.
  9252  // - `description`: The description and remediation steps for the finding.
  9253  // - `datadog_link`: The Datadog relative link for the finding.
  9254  // - `ip_addresses`: The list of private IP addresses for the resource related to the finding.
  9255  //
  9256  // ### Response
  9257  //
  9258  // The response includes an array of finding objects, pagination metadata, and a count of items that match the query.
  9259  //
  9260  // Each finding object contains the following:
  9261  //
  9262  // - The finding ID that can be used in a `GetFinding` request to retrieve the full finding details.
  9263  // - Core attributes, including status, evaluation, high-level resource details, muted state, and rule details.
  9264  // - `evaluation_changed_at` and `resource_discovery_date` time stamps.
  9265  // - An array of associated tags.

  9917  // ListSampleLogGenerationSubscriptions Get sample log generation subscriptions.
  9918  // Get the sample log generation subscriptions for the organization.

  9920  // which can be used to test detection rules without onboarding the underlying integration first.

 10078  // ListScannedAssetsMetadata List scanned assets metadata.
 10079  // Get a list of security scanned assets metadata for an organization.
 10080  //
 10081  // ### Pagination
 10082  //
 10083  // For the "List Vulnerabilities" endpoint, see the [Pagination section](#pagination).
 10084  //
 10085  // ### Filtering
 10086  //
 10087  // For the "List Vulnerabilities" endpoint, see the [Filtering section](#filtering).
 10088  //
 10089  // ### Metadata
 10090  //
 10091  //	For the "List Vulnerabilities" endpoint, see the [Metadata section](#metadata).
 10092  //
 10093  // ### Related endpoints
 10094  //
 10095  // This endpoint returns additional metadata for cloud resources that is not available from the standard resource endpoints. To access a richer dataset, call this endpoint together with the relevant resource endpoint(s) and merge (join) their results using the resource identifier.
 10096  //
 10097  // **Hosts**
 10098  //
 10099  // To enrich host data, join the response from the [Hosts](https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/hosts/) endpoint with the response from the scanned-assets-metadata endpoint on the following key fields:
 10100  //
 10101  // | ENDPOINT | JOIN KEY | TYPE |
 10102  // | --- | --- | --- |
 10103  // | [/api/v1/hosts](https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/hosts/) | host_list.host_name | string |
 10104  // | /api/v2/security/scanned-assets-metadata | data.attributes.asset.name | string |
 10105  //
 10106  // **Host Images**
 10107  //
 10108  // To enrich host image data, join the response from the [Hosts](https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/hosts/) endpoint with the response from the scanned-assets-metadata endpoint on the following key fields:
 10109  //
 10110  // | ENDPOINT | JOIN KEY | TYPE |
 10111  // | --- | --- | --- |
 10112  // | [/api/v1/hosts](https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/hosts/) | host_list.tags_by_source["Amazon Web Services"]["image"] | string |
 10113  // | /api/v2/security/scanned-assets-metadata | data.attributes.asset.name | string |
 10114  //
 10115  // **Container Images**
 10116  //
 10117  // To enrich container image data, join the response from the [Container Images](https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/container-images/) endpoint with the response from the scanned-assets-metadata endpoint on the following key fields:
 10118  //
 10119  // | ENDPOINT | JOIN KEY | TYPE |
 10120  // | --- | --- | --- |
 10121  // | [/api/v2/container_images](https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/container-images/) | `data.attributes.name`@`data.attributes.repo_digest` | string |
 10122  // | /api/v2/security/scanned-assets-metadata | data.attributes.asset.name | string |

 11381  // ListSecurityMonitoringIntegrationConfigs List entity context sync configurations.
 11382  // List the entity context sync configurations for Cloud SIEM. Each configuration connects Cloud SIEM
 11383  // to an external source that provides entities (for example, users from an identity provider) for use
 11384  // in signals and the entity explorer.

 12370  
 12371  // ListVulnerabilities List vulnerabilities.
 12372  // Get a list of vulnerabilities.
 12373  //
 12374  // ### Pagination
 12375  //
 12376  // Pagination is enabled by default in both `vulnerabilities` and `assets`. The size of the page varies depending on the endpoint and cannot be modified. To automate the request of the next page, you can use the links section in the response.
 12377  //
 12378  // This endpoint will return paginated responses. The pages are stored in the links section of the response:
 12379  //
 12380  // ```JSON
 12381  //
 12382  //	{
 12383  //	  "data": [...],
 12384  //	  "meta": {...},
 12385  //	  "links": {
 12386  //	    "self": "https://.../api/v2/security/vulnerabilities",
 12387  //	    "first": "https://.../api/v2/security/vulnerabilities?page[number]=1&page[token]=abc",
 12388  //	    "last": "https://.../api/v2/security/vulnerabilities?page[number]=43&page[token]=abc",
 12389  //	    "next": "https://.../api/v2/security/vulnerabilities?page[number]=2&page[token]=abc"
 12390  //	  }
 12391  //	}
 12392  //
 12393  // ```
 12394  //
 12395  // - `links.previous` is empty if the first page is requested.
 12396  // - `links.next` is empty if the last page is requested.
 12397  //
 12398  // #### Token
 12399  //
 12400  // Vulnerabilities can be created, updated or deleted at any point in time.
 12401  //
 12402  // Upon the first request, a token is created to ensure consistency across subsequent paginated requests.
 12403  //
 12404  // A token is valid only for 24 hours.
 12405  //
 12406  // #### First request
 12407  //
 12408  // We consider a request to be the first request when there is no `page[token]` parameter.
 12409  //
 12410  // The response of this first request contains the newly created token in the `links` section.
 12411  //
 12412  // This token can then be used in the subsequent paginated requests.
 12413  //
 12414  // *Note: The first request may take longer to complete than subsequent requests.*
 12415  //
 12416  // #### Subsequent requests
 12417  //
 12418  // Any request containing valid `page[token]` and `page[number]` parameters will be considered a subsequent request.
 12419  //
 12420  // If the `token` is invalid, a `404` response will be returned.
 12421  //
 12422  // If the page `number` is invalid, a `400` response will be returned.
 12423  //
 12424  // The returned `token` is valid for all requests in the pagination sequence. To send paginated requests in parallel, reuse the same `token` and change only the `page[number]` parameter.
 12425  //
 12426  // ### Filtering
 12427  //
 12428  // The request can include some filter parameters to filter the data to be retrieved. The format of the filter parameters follows the [JSON:API format](https://jsonapi.org/format/#fetching-filtering): `filter[$prop_name]`, where `prop_name` is the property name in the entity being filtered by.
 12429  //
 12430  // All filters can include multiple values, where data will be filtered with an OR clause: `filter[title]=Title1,Title2` will filter all vulnerabilities where title is equal to `Title1` OR `Title2`.
 12431  //
 12432  // String filters are case sensitive.
 12433  //
 12434  // Boolean filters accept `true` or `false` as values.
 12435  //
 12436  // Number filters must include an operator as a second filter input: `filter[$prop_name][$operator]`. For example, for the vulnerabilities endpoint: `filter[cvss.base.score][lte]=8`.
 12437  //
 12438  // Available operators are: `eq` (==), `lt` (<), `lte` (<=), `gt` (>) and `gte` (>=).
 12439  //
 12440  // ### Metadata
 12441  //
 12442  // Following [JSON:API format](https://jsonapi.org/format/#document-meta), object including non-standard meta-information.
 12443  //
 12444  // This endpoint includes the meta member in the response. For more details on each of the properties included in this section, check the endpoints response tables.
 12445  //
 12446  // ```JSON
 12447  //
 12448  //	{
 12449  //	  "data": [...],
 12450  //	  "meta": {
 12451  //	    "total": 1500,
 12452  //	    "count": 18732,
 12453  //	    "token": "some_token"
 12454  //	  },
 12455  //	  "links": {...}
 12456  //	}
 12457  //
 12458  // ```
 12459  // ### Extensions
 12460  //
 12461  // Requests may include extensions to modify the behavior of the requested endpoint. The filter parameters follow the [JSON:API format](https://jsonapi.org/extensions/#extensions) format: `ext:$extension_name`, where `extension_name` is the name of the modifier that is being applied.
 12462  //
 12463  // Extensions can only include one value: `ext:modifier=value`.
 12464  //
 12465  // Deprecated: This API is deprecated.

 12990  
 12991  // MuteSecurityFindings Mute or unmute security findings.
 12992  // Mute or unmute security findings.
 12993  // You can mute or unmute up to 100 security findings per request. The request body must include `is_muted` and `reason` attributes. The allowed reasons depend on whether the finding is being muted or unmuted:
 12994  //   - To mute a finding: `PENDING_FIX`, `FALSE_POSITIVE`, `OTHER`, `NO_FIX`, `DUPLICATE`, `RISK_ACCEPTED`.
 12995  //   - To unmute a finding: `NO_PENDING_FIX`, `HUMAN_ERROR`, `NO_LONGER_ACCEPTED_RISK`, `OTHER`.

 13840  	}

 13842  	items := make(chan datadog.PaginationResult[SecurityFindingsData], pageSize_)
 13843  	go func() {
 13844  		for {
 13845  			resp, _, err := a.SearchSecurityFindings(ctx, body)
 13846  			if err != nil {
 13847  				var returnItem SecurityFindingsData
 13848  				items <- datadog.PaginationResult[SecurityFindingsData]{Item: returnItem, Error: err}

 13851  			respData, ok := resp.GetDataOk()
 13852  			if !ok {
 13853  				break
 13854  			}
 13855  			results := *respData
 13856  
 13857  			for _, item := range results {
 13858  				select {
 13859  				case items <- datadog.PaginationResult[SecurityFindingsData]{Item: item, Error: nil}:
 13860  				case <-ctx.Done():
 13861  					close(items)
 13862  					return
 13863  				}
 13864  			}
 13865  			if len(results) == 0 {
 13866  				break
 13867  			}
 13868  			cursorMeta, ok := resp.GetMetaOk()
 13869  			if !ok {
 13870  				break
 13871  			}
 13872  			cursorMetaPage, ok := cursorMeta.GetPageOk()
 13873  			if !ok {
 13874  				break
 13875  			}
 13876  			cursorMetaPageAfter, ok := cursorMetaPage.GetAfterOk()
 13877  			if !ok {
 13878  				break
 13879  			}
 13880  
 13881  			body.Data.Attributes.Page.Cursor = cursorMetaPageAfter
 13882  		}
 13883  		close(items)
 13884  	}()
 13885  	return items, cancel
 13886  }
